title: On the moral economy of the corner shop in a city of chains
summary: Argue that the disappearance of the independent corner shop is not merely a commercial event but the loss of a small civic infrastructure whose moral functions chains do not replicate.
topic: What is lost when chain stores replace the independent neighbourhood shop
prompt: Write a 600–800-word essay arguing that the independent corner shop performs moral and civic functions that are invisible to commercial accounting and therefore vanish when it is replaced by a chain. Avoid nostalgia for its own sake; treat the shop as a specific kind of urban institution. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'Granted… Even so…' concession. Set examples in real cities (Lisbon, Istanbul, Hanoi, etc.). Avoid the rhetoric of 'shop local' campaigns.
hintStarters: Only rarely is the closure of a small shop registered as anything more than a commercial event…
What the language of efficiency cannot account for is the function of a place that has no formal function at all…
Granted, chains offer lower prices, longer hours, and a wider range. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: không gian thứ ba — không phải nhà, không phải nơi làm việc
mối quan hệ yếu, không thân thiết
áp lực tiền thuê thương mại
người không có tài khoản ngân hàng
tấm vải xã hội, kết cấu xã hội
những hành động tin cậy nhỏ

sampleText:
Only rarely is the closure of a small shop registered as anything more than a commercial event. A sign comes down, a chain replaces it, the prices fall, the hours extend, and the street appears, by every measure the council records, to have been improved. What disappears is not in the accounts. It is the proprietor who knew which of the elderly residents had not been seen for two days, the tab that kept a household in milk during the last week of a difficult month, the corner from which a slow conversation was conducted, every morning, by people who otherwise had no occasion to address one another. None of this appears on the rent roll. It does not need to. Its absence will appear later, distributed across other ledgers — clinic visits not made in time, isolations not noticed — and by then the connection will be hard to trace.

It is easy to overstate what the corner shop was. The literature of local commerce can curdle quickly into sentimentalism, and most of us have known proprietors who were rude, prices that were inflated, and shops whose dust deserved their closure. The argument is not that every small shop was a moral institution. It is that some were, and that the conditions which allowed them to be — long tenure, a real proprietor on the floor, a customer base small enough to be remembered — are precisely the conditions that chain commerce, by design, abolishes. The chain does not refuse to recognise its customers; it is structurally incapable of doing so.

What the language of efficiency cannot account for is the function of a place that has no formal function at all. A neighbourhood needs surfaces on which weak ties can form — the brief encounter, the half-conversation, the mutual recognition between people who would never invite each other to dinner. Sociologists since Granovetter have known that these weak ties carry disproportionate informational and emotional weight; they are how news of a job reaches a stranger, how a recently widowed neighbour is checked on by someone who is not family, how a child who has wandered too far is returned. Such ties do not require a corner shop. But they require some surface on which they can settle, and in many cities — Lisbon, Istanbul, Hanoi, Naples — that surface was, for a long time, the local trader.

It is the asymmetry of what is lost, not the absence of compensating goods, that gives the displacement its particular moral character. Granted, chains offer lower prices, longer hours, and a wider range. Even so, the costs and the benefits do not fall on the same people. The shopper who can drive to a supermarket on the edge of the city benefits from its prices; the elderly woman on the fourth floor without a lift, who used to send a child down to the corner with a note, does not. The young office worker who shops late benefits from the extended hours; the unbanked tenant who relied on an informal tab loses a small credit line that no commercial product replaces. The benefit is broadly distributed and visible. The loss is concentrated, unmeasured, and tends to fall on those least able to publicise it.

There is also something more difficult to name. A neighbourhood in which one is recognised — addressed by name, asked after, missed when absent — is not the same neighbourhood as one in which one is anonymous. The recognition that the corner shop provided was not deep; it was, at most, a small civic warmth. But it is the kind of warmth on which a city's tolerance for its own strangers quietly depends. A neighbourhood that no longer recognises anyone has fewer reasons not to suspect everyone. The slide from a city of named customers to a city of ambient surveillance is shorter than it appears.

None of this is an argument against chains, exactly. It is an argument for noticing, before the next closure, what we are about to subtract. The corner shop was rarely magnificent and often not worth preserving on its own terms. The functions it carried were. Whether we choose to rebuild those functions in some other form, or to let them quietly disappear and discover the cost later, is a decision the rent market will not make for us. It will only present the bill.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive inversion with 'Only rarely' for an understated opening claim
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở đầu 'Only rarely is the closure of a small shop registered as anything more than a commercial event' đảo trợ động từ 'is' trước chủ ngữ 'the closure of a small shop' sau cụm phủ định 'Only rarely'. Cấu trúc bị động kết hợp với đảo ngữ tạo nên ấn tượng về quan sát khách quan, có khoảng cách — đúng giọng điệu lập luận của bài.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Only rarely is the closure of a small shop registered as anything more than a commercial event' inverts the auxiliary 'is' before the subject after the negative adverbial 'Only rarely'. Combining inversion with passive voice gives an impersonal, observational tone — appropriate for an essay diagnosing a structural phenomenon.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence with 'It is X, not Y, that Z' to name the real moral wrong
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the asymmetry of what is lost, not the absence of compensating goods, that gives the displacement its particular moral character' dùng cleft để chuyển trọng tâm phê phán: bài không phủ nhận rằng có lợi ích bù đắp, mà chỉ ra rằng vấn đề là ai được lợi và ai chịu thiệt. Cleft thực hiện chính sự chuyển dời lập luận đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the asymmetry of what is lost, not the absence of compensating goods, that gives the displacement its particular moral character' is a cleft that does not deny the compensating benefits — it relocates the moral problem to the asymmetry of who gains and who loses. The cleft enacts the essay's central reframing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chỉ hiếm khi việc đóng cửa của một cửa hàng nhỏ được ghi nhận như bất cứ điều gì khác ngoài một sự kiện thương mại. Một biển hiệu được tháo xuống, một chuỗi thay thế nó, giá giảm, giờ kéo dài, và con phố dường như, theo mọi thước đo mà hội đồng ghi nhận, đã được cải thiện. Những gì biến mất không có trong các tài khoản. Đó là người chủ biết cư dân lớn tuổi nào đã không xuất hiện trong hai ngày, sổ ghi nợ giúp một hộ gia đình có sữa trong tuần cuối của một tháng khó khăn. Không có gì trong số này xuất hiện trên sổ tiền thuê. Sự vắng mặt của nó sẽ xuất hiện sau, phân tán trên các sổ cái khác — những lần khám bệnh không kịp, những sự cô lập không được nhận ra.

Dễ phóng đại những gì cửa hàng góc phố từng là. Tài liệu về thương mại địa phương có thể nhanh chóng đông lại thành chủ nghĩa tình cảm. Lập luận không phải là mọi cửa hàng nhỏ đều là thể chế đạo đức. Mà là một số đã từng, và rằng những điều kiện cho phép chúng trở nên như vậy — thời gian thuê dài, một người chủ thật trên sàn, một lượng khách đủ nhỏ để được nhớ — là chính những điều kiện mà thương mại chuỗi, theo thiết kế, xoá bỏ. Chuỗi không phải từ chối công nhận khách hàng của mình; nó không có khả năng làm điều đó về mặt cấu trúc.

Những gì ngôn ngữ hiệu quả không thể tính đến là chức năng của một nơi không có chức năng chính thức nào cả. Một khu phố cần những bề mặt mà các mối quan hệ yếu có thể hình thành — cuộc gặp ngắn, cuộc trò chuyện nửa chừng, sự công nhận lẫn nhau giữa những người sẽ không bao giờ mời nhau ăn tối. Các nhà xã hội học từ thời Granovetter đã biết rằng các mối quan hệ yếu mang trọng lượng thông tin và cảm xúc lớn không tương xứng. Những mối quan hệ như vậy không yêu cầu một cửa hàng góc phố. Nhưng chúng yêu cầu một bề mặt nào đó để định cư, và ở nhiều thành phố — Lisbon, Istanbul, Hà Nội, Naples — bề mặt đó, trong một thời gian dài, là người buôn bán địa phương.

Chính sự bất đối xứng của những gì bị mất, không phải sự vắng mặt của các lợi ích bù đắp, mới mang lại cho sự dịch chuyển đặc tính đạo đức riêng biệt. Cứ cho là chuỗi cung cấp giá thấp hơn, giờ mở dài hơn, và phạm vi rộng hơn. Dù vậy, chi phí và lợi ích không rơi vào cùng những người. Người mua hàng có thể lái xe đến siêu thị ở rìa thành phố được hưởng lợi từ giá; người phụ nữ lớn tuổi ở tầng bốn không có thang máy, từng sai một đứa trẻ xuống góc với một mảnh giấy, thì không. Lợi ích được phân phối rộng và dễ thấy. Tổn thất tập trung, không được đo lường, và có xu hướng rơi vào những người ít có khả năng công bố nhất.

Cũng có điều gì đó khó đặt tên hơn. Một khu phố nơi người ta được công nhận — được gọi tên, được hỏi thăm, được nhớ khi vắng mặt — không phải là cùng một khu phố với nơi người ta là vô danh. Sự công nhận mà cửa hàng góc phố cung cấp không sâu; nó, nhiều nhất, là một sự ấm áp dân sinh nhỏ. Nhưng đó là loại ấm áp mà sự khoan dung của một thành phố đối với những người lạ của chính nó âm thầm phụ thuộc vào. Một khu phố không còn công nhận bất kỳ ai có ít lý do hơn để không nghi ngờ mọi người.

Không có gì ở đây là lập luận chống lại chuỗi, chính xác. Đó là lập luận cho việc nhận ra, trước lần đóng cửa tiếp theo, những gì chúng ta sắp trừ bớt. Cửa hàng góc phố hiếm khi tráng lệ và thường không đáng được bảo tồn trên các điều khoản của chính nó. Các chức năng nó mang theo thì đáng. Liệu chúng ta có chọn xây dựng lại các chức năng đó dưới một hình thức khác, hay để chúng âm thầm biến mất và phát hiện ra chi phí sau này, là một quyết định mà thị trường tiền thuê sẽ không quyết định thay chúng ta. Nó chỉ trình hoá đơn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies passive inversion after 'Only rarely'?
options: Only rarely the closure of a small shop is registered as a commercial event.
Only rarely registered as a commercial event is the closure of a small shop.
The closure of a small shop only rarely registers as a commercial event.
Only rarely is the closure of a small shop registered as anything more than a commercial event.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Only rarely', the passive auxiliary 'is' must precede the subject 'the closure of a small shop'. Without inversion, the negative fronting is ungrammatical at C1 register.
mcQuestions.hint: Negative fronting + passive: auxiliary 'is' precedes subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central claim about what disappears when a corner shop closes?
options: Civic and moral functions that do not appear in commercial accounting, but whose absence shows up later in other ledgers
Cheap goods that the chain cannot match in price
Aesthetic charm valued mainly by tourists
Local employment for shop assistants
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that the loss is invisible to commercial accounting and reappears later as clinic visits missed, isolations unnoticed — a moral and civic loss disguised as a commercial event.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the first paragraph's image of the absent ledger.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say chain commerce is 'structurally incapable' of recognising customers?
options: Because chain employees are deliberately rude
Because the conditions that make recognition possible — long tenure, a real proprietor, a small remembered customer base — are abolished by the chain's design
Because chains use software to anonymise customer data
Because chain managers refuse to learn local languages
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay carefully distinguishes refusal from structural impossibility: the chain's design — high turnover, no proprietor, large undifferentiated customer base — makes recognition impossible by construction.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'structurally incapable' and what conditions it follows from.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the role of 'weak ties' in the essay's argument?
options: They are the strong intimate bonds of family and close friendship
They are the legal ties between landlords and tenants
They are brief, non-intimate connections that carry disproportionate informational and emotional weight for cities and neighbourhoods
They are temporary employment contracts in informal markets
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay cites the sociologist Granovetter: weak ties are non-intimate connections that nonetheless do disproportionate work in cities — spreading information, providing checks on isolation, returning lost children.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to name the real moral character of the displacement?
options: The absence of compensating goods gives the displacement its moral character.
Asymmetry, not compensation, gives the displacement its moral force.
What gives the displacement moral character is the lack of compensation.
It is the asymmetry of what is lost, not the absence of compensating goods, that gives the displacement its particular moral character.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that Z' is a cleft that does not deny compensating goods — it relocates the moral problem to who gains and who loses. C1 essays use clefts to reframe.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is X, not Y, that Z' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's response to the chain's advantages of lower prices and longer hours?
options: It concedes the advantages but observes that the benefits and the costs fall on different populations — the asymmetry is the moral issue
It denies that chains offer such advantages
It argues that the longer hours are illusory
It claims that all shoppers prefer corner shops if asked
mcQuestions.explanation: The Granted/Even so move concedes the advantages of chains but argues that the beneficiaries and the losers are not the same people — the elderly resident on the fourth floor, the unbanked tenant, do not benefit.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the Granted/Even so paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'a small civic warmth'?
options: Physical heating in a shop
A modest form of public recognition — being addressed by name, asked after, missed when absent — that is small but on which a city's tolerance for strangers quietly depends
A sentimental memory of childhood neighbourhoods
An exaggerated metaphor for retail customer service
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes the recognition the shop provides as 'small' but argues that it is the kind of warmth a city's tolerance for its strangers depends on — small in degree, structurally important.
mcQuestions.hint: Look near the end for what 'small civic warmth' enables.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's view of 'sentimentalism' about local commerce?
options: It is the only honest way to remember corner shops
It is necessary for political mobilisation
It is a risk the essay explicitly disavows — not every corner shop was a moral institution, and the argument depends on the specific functions, not on nostalgia
It is identical to the argument the essay makes
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes 'most of us have known proprietors who were rude, prices that were inflated, and shops whose dust deserved their closure' — the argument turns on specific functions, not romanticism.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the paragraph beginning 'It is easy to overstate…'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which group does the essay name as a likely loser when chains replace corner shops?
options: Young office workers who shop late
Tourists visiting historical districts
Suppliers of imported goods
The unbanked tenant who relied on an informal tab — and the elderly resident without a lift who used to send a child to the corner
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay names two specific losers: the unbanked tenant (loses informal credit) and the elderly resident without a lift (loses local accessibility). The young office worker is named as a beneficiary, not a loser.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the slide from a city of named customers to a city of ambient surveillance'?
options: When neighbours no longer recognise each other, the recognition that holds a city together is replaced by undifferentiated suspicion of strangers and ambient monitoring
Cities will become safer through CCTV in shops
Customers prefer to remain anonymous in modern cities
Surveillance technology is the inevitable future of all retail
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that a neighbourhood that no longer recognises anyone has 'fewer reasons not to suspect everyone' — the loss of small recognition slides into systemic suspicion.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at 'fewer reasons not to suspect everyone'.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay frame the role of 'the local witness'?
options: A legal observer in courtroom proceedings
The proprietor or regular shopkeeper who notices when someone has not been seen for two days — a small but functional check on isolation
A journalist reporting on neighbourhood events
A government inspector visiting local shops
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's image of the proprietor 'who knew which of the elderly residents had not been seen for two days' is exactly this role — a quiet, informal witness whose vigilance is a civic function.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the first paragraph image of the proprietor.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'informal credit' refer to in the essay?
options: A bank loan with low interest
Credit cards issued informally without paperwork
A small line of credit extended by a known shopkeeper — the 'tab' — to a customer in difficulty, on the strength of personal knowledge
Government welfare paid in cash
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes 'the tab that kept a household in milk during the last week of a difficult month' — an unwritten credit line backed by personal recognition, which no chain replicates.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'tab' in the first paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about who is least able to publicise their loss?
options: Wealthy investors who funded the corner shop
Journalists who report on commercial change
Council officials responsible for licensing
Those most affected — elderly, isolated, unbanked — tend to be those least able to make their loss visible in public debate
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay observes that the loss is 'concentrated, unmeasured, and tends to fall on those least able to publicise it' — a political asymmetry that makes the harm hard to register.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the asymmetry paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's closing image — 'the rent market will not make for us. It will only present the bill' — mean?
options: Markets will not decide which civic functions to preserve; they only present the consequences after the decision has been made by default
Rent will continue to rise indefinitely
Council tax should be lowered for small shops
The bill for civic loss is paid by chains
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing image distinguishes the market from a decision-making body: it does not choose civic outcomes, it merely registers, after the fact, what we have allowed to be lost.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final two sentences.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which best characterises the essay's overall stance?
options: An argument that chains should be banned
An argument that what we lose with corner shops is civic infrastructure that markets cannot account for, requiring deliberate choice about whether to rebuild it
A call for state subsidy of all small shops
A nostalgic lament for a vanished urban past
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay is careful to disavow both nostalgia and prohibition; its claim is that the civic functions need to be deliberately noticed and, if valued, rebuilt by other means — markets alone will not preserve them.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the closing paragraph's framing of the choice.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a small shop, café, or local trader in your own city whose closure would constitute more than a commercial event? What civic function does it perform that would not be replaced by the chain or platform that took its place? And whose responsibility, if anyone's, is it to preserve such functions?
